data.title: FCC: Late-night show may not be exempt from equal time for opposing candidates
data.description:
The FCC is changing its rules exempting certain late night and daytime talk shows from being required to provide equal airtime to opposing political candidates.

“Importantly, the FCC has not been presented with any evidence that the interview portion of any late night or daytime television talk show program on air presently would qualify for the bona fide news exemption,” the FCC said in a public notice made on Wednesday. “Moreover, a program that is motivated by partisan purposes, for example, would not be entitled to an exemption under longstanding FCC precedent.”

The move comes as Brendan Carr, who was appointed by President Trump, has criticized a number of leading late night and daytime talk shows on major networks like ABC, NBC and CBS.

Carr has said those programs, because they air over a broadcast network rather than a cable channel or streamer, have an obligation to serve the “public interest,” and the FCC intends to hold their networks to that standard.

But critics of the administration argue Trump is using Carr’s FCC to crack down on news and commentary that is critical of his policies and stifling free speech.

During a pair of Congressional hearings in recent weeks, Carr has defended the Trump administration’s FCC policies and argued before lawmakers that his department is not technically an independent government agency.

“It is important that both broadcasters and legally qualified candidates understand the FCC’s equal opportunities regulations and how they can result in broadcasters offering opposing legal qualified candidates comparable time and placement,” the department said in its public notice.

data.title: Trump acts like an imperial bully-in-chief
data.description:
President Trump’s view of international relations can be summarized in three words: “Might makes right.”

Trump clearly agrees with his deputy chief of staff, Stephen Miller, who recently told CNN: “We live in a world … that is governed by strength, that is governed by force, that is governed by power.”

Trump is turning back the clock to the days of imperialism, when the Roman Empire and later England, France, Spain and other great powers — followed by Nazi Germany, the Japanese Empire, fascist Italy and the Soviet Union — imposed their will on weaker adversaries by force.

Americans used force to enslave millions of Africans. America seized land from Native Americans, take what is now the Western U.S. from Mexico, and conquer other territories. And Trump wants to bring back those days of unrestrained American power and expansionism.

He is using the U.S. military to demand other nations do whatever he commands, as if he is emperor of the world. He arrogantly ignores limits that U.S. law, the Constitution and international law place on what he can do.

Asked in a recent New York Times interview if there are any limits on actions he can take to impose his will around the world, Trump responded: “Yeah, there is one thing. My own morality. My own mind. It’s the only thing that can stop me.”

“I don’t need international law,” Trump added. “I’m not looking to hurt people.”

Trump recently ordered the abduction and imprisonment of Nicolas Maduro and his wife in a raid that Venezuela says killed more than 100 people. He claims the right to run that country and control its oil for years, even outrageously calling himself Venezuela’s “acting president” on social media. He had earlier ordered attacks on boats near Venezuela that were allegedly carrying illegal drugs, killing more than 100 people on board without any attempt to arrest them.

Trump’s obsession with grabbing Venezuela’s oil and encouraging more domestic fossil fuel production — while doing all he can to limit the development of clean energy in the U.S. — makes no sense. But he refuses to believe that harmful climate change is caused by oil, natural gas and coal emissions, calling the scientific consensus a “con job,” a “hoax” and a “scam.”

Trump has demanded that Canada become the 51st U.S. state. He says America will retake control of the Panama Canal. He says the U.S. must control Greenland, a semiautonomous territory of NATO member Denmark. Until this week, he had been refusing to take military force off the table to achieve his goal.

Trump has attacked Iran and threatens to do so again. He has ordered attacks on Islamist terrorists in Syria and Nigeria. He has made threats against Cuba, Colombia and Mexico, asserting that the U.S. has the right to dominate the Western Hemisphere under an updated Monroe Doctrine that he calls the Donroe Doctrine.

He has also said America should take ownership of the Gaza Strip and turn it into a tourist resort.

Whatever happened to Trump’s campaign promise to get out of foreign wars and not start new ones? Is he not concerned that his military adventurism could trigger retaliatory strikes and terrorist attacks by his targets that would kill U.S. military members and civilians at home and abroad?

Trump’s aggressive actions could encourage Russian President Vladimir Putin to use military force to conquer not just Ukraine, but other nations once part of the Soviet Union. Chinese President Xi Jinping could be encouraged to invade Taiwan and intimidate other Asian nations.

We don’t know how Trump would respond to such attacks by strongmen he has openly admired and tried to emulate with his own ruthless exercise of power.

Following the deaths of tens of millions of people in World War II and the development of nuclear weapons, nations around the world established the United Nations and strengthened efforts to use international law to limit future conflicts and promote global cooperation. Although there have been many new wars, we have thankfully been spared from World War III.

But Trump has frequently denounced the U.N. and recently withdrawn the U.S. from 31 U.N. agencies and dozens of other global organizations. He has eroded our moral standing in the world by eliminating almost all funding for the U.S. Agency for International Development, which had provided lifesaving medical and food aid and saved millions of lives in poor nations.

Trump has made the world a more dangerous place with incompetent, erratic and dangerous policies based more on his emotions, ego, personal business interests and ignorance than on America’s strategic interests. He has alienated our allies by acting as the global bully in chief and embraced our enemies, most notably Putin.

“Might makes right,” though immoral, anti-democratic and destructive, was the dominant form of international relations for thousands of years. It is frightening that the president of the most powerful nation on Earth seeks to abandon the progress of the post-World War II era, return the world to the deadly and barbaric law of the jungle, and exercise tyrannical control over weaker nations. We deserve better from our president.

A. Scott Bolden is an attorney, NewsNation contributor, former chair of the Washington, D.C. Democratic Party and a former New York state prosecutor.

data.title: Key panel advances three-bill funding package
data.description:
The House Rules Committee on Thursday advanced a three-bill minibus appropriations package, moving the lower chamber one step closer to meeting the Jan. 30 funding deadline.

The panel advanced the package by a recorded vote of 9-4. It would fund the departments of Defense, Housing and Urban Development, Health and Human Services, Labor, Education, Transportation and other related agencies. 

It will now head to the floor for consideration.

During the House Rules Committee hearing on Wednesday, Democrats had raised concerns that the Defense bill in the minibus package didn’t address President Trump’s ongoing threats against Greenland.

Trump has long sought to take over Greenland, a semiautonomous territory of Denmark, and in recent weeks has floated the use of military force to do so. But he reversed course on Wednesday and suggested that the U.S. would not seize the territory by force. He instead called for immediate negotiations for the U.S. to acquire Greenland.

Rep. Jim McGovern (D-Mass.), the ranking member of the Rules Committee, said that the Defense bill was “maybe the last opportunity to prevent this administration from, like, doing something crazy in Greenland or, you know, attacking NATO.”

“I really think that we’re at a moment where we all have to recognize that some of the stuff he’s saying is not only dangerous, it’s just way out there. It’s not normal. And so, yeah, a lot of us believe that just to be safe, there ought to be constraints” in the bill, McGovern said.

Amendments offered by Democrats that sought to address the issue ultimately did not advance for floor consideration.

Internal Republican disputes had also stalled the bills’ advancement through the House Rules Committee on Wednesday.

One major issue GOP leaders faced was requests from midwestern Republicans to authorize year-round sales of E15 ethanol, a fuel blend with 10.5 percent to 15 percent ethanol blended with gasoline that has historically been restricted in the summer due to concerns about smog. 

But GOP leadership reached a deal with the holdouts to create a rural domestic energy council tasking with addressing the authorization of E15 ethanol. The council will be established if the procedural rule on the minibus is adopted. 

Some hard-line conservatives also voiced objections to the package, particularly the earmarks included for various community projects and organizations in local districts. 

“The U.S. is NEARLY $38.5 TRILLION in debt. Families have been cutting back while Washington isn’t,” Rep. Ralph Norman (R-S.C.), who sits on the committee, wrote on X. 

Norman eventually had one of his amendments made in order that would prohibit “funds for certain community funding projects.”

But the package is expected to pass the House with broad bipartisan support. It had been strongly endorsed by House Appropriations Committee Chair Tom Cole (R-Okla.) and ranking member Rosa DeLauro (D-Conn.).

“This latest funding package continues Congress’s forceful rejection of extreme cuts to federal programs proposed by the Trump Administration,” DeLauro said in a statement. “Where the White House attempted to eliminate entire programs, we chose to increase their funding. Where the Administration proposed slashing resources, we chose to sustain funding at current levels.”

Cole said during the House Rules hearing that, “Across these measures, we have real deliverables and real wins.”

data.title: Former Iowa superintendent expected to plead guilty to false citizenship claim
data.description:
Former Des Moines, Iowa, schools Superintendent Ian Roberts is expected to plead guilty Thursday in federal court to charges he made false citizenship claims in employment and illegally possessed firearms.  

Roberts was arrested in September by Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), with agents saying he had a removal order since May 2024 and was found in possession of a loaded handgun, which is a crime for those without legal status.

Court documents show Roberts is prepared to plead guilty to lying about his citizenship on employment papers and possession of multiple loaded guns, one in his car and three others at his residence.  

The Hill has reached out to the Department of Homeland Security for comment. 

Roberts, a former Olympic athlete who competed for Guyana, began his two-year tenure as superintendent of Des Moines Public Schools in 2023, where school officials said they had no idea he lied about his citizenship status.  

Before this role, he worked in the education sector in multiple states for two decades. 

Alfredo Parrish, one of Roberts’s attorneys, told the Associated Press that Roberts thought his case was resolved by a previous attorney. 

If convicted, Roberts could face up to 20 years of prison time and be deported after his sentence.

data.title: Smith to condemn 'false' narratives' about Trump investigations
data.description:
Former special counsel Jack Smith will stand by his decision to bring charges against President Trump and “false” narratives around that decision during his public testimony before the House Judiciary Committee Thursday, according to multiple reports. 

“Our investigation developed proof beyond a reasonable doubt that President Trump engaged in criminal activity,” Smith is expected to say Thursday in his opening statement, according to The Associated Press. “If asked whether to prosecute a former president based on the same facts today, I would do so regardless of whether that president was a Republican or a Democrat.”

Smith, an appointee of former President Biden, is testifying at the request of Judiciary Committee Chair Jim Jordan (R-Ohio) on his probes into Trump’s alleged efforts to overturn the 2020 election and his mishandling of classified documents. The hearing is set to begin at 10 a.m. EST.

“No one should be above the law in our country, and the law required that [Trump] be held to account. So that is what I did,” Smith will say, per the AP.

The former prosecutor previously testified for over seven hours in front of the committee on Dec. 17. Jordan’s team released a 255-page transcript of Smith’s deposition on New Year’s Eve.

Throughout that testimony, Smith said he had a strong case against the president. Both investigations, which resulted in the president being indicted in the District of Columbia and Southern District of Florida, were dropped after Trump was elected in 2024. 

At the time, Smith cited the precedent of not bringing charges against a sitting president.

“Our investigation developed proof beyond a reasonable doubt that President Trump engaged in a criminal scheme to overturn the results of the 2020 election and to prevent the lawful transfer of power,” Smith testified in December. “Our investigation also developed powerful evidence that showed that President Trump willfully retained highly classified documents after he left office in January of 2021, storing them at his social club, including in a ballroom and a bathroom. 

“He then repeatedly tried to obstruct justice to conceal his continued retention of those documents,” the former special counsel added.

The public has only seen Smith’s report about his investigation into Trump’s attempts to overturn the 2020 election and block the transfer of power on Jan. 6, 2021. The final report into the 300 classified documents recovered from the president’s Mar-a-Lago estate in Florida remains private.

data.title: Prince Harry accuses tabloids of making Meghan's life an 'absolute misery'
data.description:
(NewsNation) — An emotional Prince Harry has called out the British tabloids for making Meghan Markle’s life terrible.

Harry testified Wednesday in London’s High Court as part of his lawsuit against Associated Newspapers Ltd., the publisher behind the Daily Mail, revealing the impact that the company has had on his life in recent years.

“They have made my wife’s life an absolute misery,” he told his lawyer, David Sherborne, during his testimony, according to NBC News.

The Duke of Sussex said his life had been “commercialized” since he was a teenager.

“The defense’s claim that I don’t have any right to privacy is disgusting,” Harry said.

Meghan Markle and Prince Harry pose for pictures after visiting the observatory in One World Trade in New York, Sept. 23, 2021. (AP Photo/Seth Wenig, File)

The prince is one of seven prominent people, including Elton John and Elizabeth Hurley, who are suing Associated Newspapers, alleging invasion of privacy and that the paper has engaged in “clear, systematic and sustained use of unlawful information gathering” for two decades, Sherborne said, according to The Associated Press.

“I think it is fundamentally wrong to have to put all of us through this again when all we were asking for is an apology and some accountability,” Harry said on the stand.

Prince Harry, Meghan Markle: We’re targets of press

Prince Harry said in a witness statement that he’s taken legal action against the publisher because of “vicious” and “sometimes racist” coverage of Meghan after the two’s relationship became public, according to People magazine.

“However, in late 2016, when my relationship with Meghan, my now wife, became public, I started to become increasingly troubled by the approach of not taking action against the press in the wake of vicious persistent attacks on, harassment of and intrusive, sometimes racist articles concerning Meghan,” he said.

He added, “The situation got worse when she became pregnant and after our son, Archie, was born.”

In January 2025, Harry scored a huge victory when Rupert Murdoch’s News Group Newspapers settled with Harry for $12 million and a public apology for invading his privacy through deceptive means, such as phone hacking.

data.title: Key panel advances Department of Homeland Security funding bill
data.description:
The House Rules Committee early Thursday advanced a controversial Department of Homeland Security (DHS) appropriations bill, clearing a key procedural hurdle and sending it to the floor for consideration.

The bill would allow tens of billions of dollars to flow into Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), a major point of contention among Democrats after an ICE officer fatally shot a woman in Minneapolis earlier this month.

It would also provide funding for the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA), the Transportation Security Administration (TSA), the U.S. Secret Service, the Disaster Relief Fund and other programs and agencies. 

The DHS bill includes a few targeted reforms aimed at appeasing Democrats, including a $115 million reduction in funding for ICE enforcement and removal operations, a decrease of 5,500 ICE detention beds and a $1.8 billion cut in Border Patrol funding. It also strengthens oversight of ICE through the Office of the Inspector General and Office for Civil Rights and Civil Liberties.

However, Democrats on the Rules Committee sharply criticized ICE’s actions, arguing that the bill does not go far enough to rein in the agency.

“Here’s the deal, not only what I have witnessed, you know, in terms of ICE behavior, by the way, not just in Minnesota, but even in Massachusetts and in other states. You know, that just is unacceptable,” House Rules Committee ranking member Jim McGovern (D-Mass.) said.

“Asking American citizens ‘papers please’ is not freedom. You know, being fearful of a heavily armed, masked you know, group of U.S. forces is not democracy,” he added. “And all of us agree that criminals and people who commit terrible crimes should be held to account. But you know, we have U.S. citizens that are being detained.”

Rep. Teresa Leger Fernández (D-N.M.), a member of the committee, said ICE should be held up to standards of “accountability,” which she argued the legislation doesn’t address. 

House Appropriations Committee ranking member Rosa DeLauro (D-Conn.) said during the hearing that she believes there are some good pieces to the bill, but it falls short in establishing the accountability, transparency, and enforcement guardrails she had sought.

She also added that it is ultimately up to her fellow Democrats to decide whether they will support the bill when it reaches the floor.

“I think everyone has to pay a close attention to what is in the bill, what they can view as positive or negative, and come to a conclusion about where they want to be on the bill,” DeLauro said. 

She had also warned in a previous statement that a shutdown would only leave TSA agents without pay and delay FEMA assistance, while a short-term stopgap measure would merely cede authority to the Trump administration.

During a closed-door meeting on Wednesday, House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) told his caucus he would vote against the DHS bill when it hits the floor. Even without a majority of Democratic support, though, the bill is still expected to pass the House.

data.title: Trump should abandon Democrats' push to ban investors from buying homes
data.description:
Earlier this month, President Trump pledged immediate “steps to ban large, institutional investors from buying more single-family homes.” Then recently, the president signed an executive order to that effect.

“People live in homes, not corporations,” he said in a Jan. 7 social media post.

Trump is not the first to point an accusatory finger at large-scale investors who rent out homes. Last year, a bill to effectively ban the activity was introduced by Sen. Jeff Merkley (D-Ore.) and sponsored by a group including Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.).

But don’t be fooled by the bipartisan ire. These homes are not the cause of America’s housing-access crisis. They are an inevitable, even necessary, response to it.

In 2008, amid the chaos of the budding financial crisis, the federal mortgage agencies, Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, and the Federal Housing Administration eliminated half of their mortgage lending to borrowers with credit scores below 740. The average U.S. credit score is just a bit above 700.

As a consequence, millions of families who would have responsibly taken out mortgages in previous decades are now blocked by tight underwriting standards. Many need homes that they now aren’t allowed to own.

Back when those families were being served by our mortgage programs, large investors were not involved at all in single-family housing markets. Being a homeowner has value, and those families were (and are) always willing to pay more for a new home than large, purely profit-driven investors are.

Since 2008, millions of families have needed deep-pocketed institutions to buy up and rent out the millions of homes they need to live in. Large investors now partly fill the void.

The squeeze is getting tighter. Even under the new lending standards, every year there are more households in our growing country, and most are homeowners. Since the end of 2015, the number of homeowners has increased by 11.4 million, but builders have only completed about 9 million new single-family homes.

This is why houses are so expensive. It’s also causing homeowners to buy existing homes away from renters.

On net, those renters are moving from houses to apartments, but municipalities also make building apartments difficult, for reasons steeped in a history of NIMBYism, classism and sometimes racism. Builders have managed to complete about 4 million new apartments since 2015. That hasn’t been nearly enough to accommodate the natural growth of new households who are renters plus the more than 2 million tenants who had to switch from single-family homes to apartments when existing rental homes were purchased by families.

As a result, there are many renter households that simply haven’t formed. Among these are young adults living with parents into their 20s and 30s. Some are absorbed into other homes as roommates or couch-surfers. Some are living in cars or tents in the park.

The story is complicated, but in the end, we have millions of renters who need homes and landlords, and the regulatory landscape is stacked against them. Banning investor-owned homes would only take one more option off the table.

Think about it another way: If you go to a neighborhood where builders are selling new homes, will you find Wall Street coming in and buying up all the houses at a premium before families can? The answer is no.

More commonly, builders are aiming for sales to families. In neighborhoods where sales are slow, they turn to investors who might buy a handful of homes, all at once with a discount — for less profit. In these neighborhoods, it is the families who are outbidding investors. Typically, builders only sell to investors to get rid of inventory families haven’t bought.

Housing is eating up the budgets of renters across the country. They desperately need Republicans and Democrats to find coherent economic solutions, not knee-jerk reactions to problems created right under their noses in Washington.

The Trump administration has control over the one lever that families used for a century to outbid Wall Street: reasonable, 20th century lending standards at the federal housing agencies. Restoring them would allow families to once again take on financial responsibility for homeownership.

It is a no-brainer. Give them permission to outbid the investors simply by paying market value. All they need is permission. Federal mortgage regulators and underwriters are the problem.

Kevin Erdmann is a senior affiliated scholar at the Mercatus Center at George Mason University.

data.title: Trump, Kushner launch Board of Peace at Davos — without EU partners
data.description:
President Trump and his administration rolled out the Board of Peace initiative for Gaza in Davos on Thursday featuring leaders of over 20 countries that notably did not include major European Union allies.

Countries that have agreed to join the board include Argentina, Belarus, Morocco, Vietnam, Pakistan, Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, Kosovo, Hungary, Egypt, Turkey, Qatar, Jordan, Indonesia and Saudi Arabia. 

The White House initially included a list on Thursday that included Belgium as a member, however the country’s Deputy Prime Minister Maxime Prevot said otherwise.

“Belgium has NOT signed the Charter of the Board of Peace. This announcement is incorrect,” Prevot wrote in a post on X. “We wish for a common and coordinated European response. As many European countries, we have reservations to the proposal.”

Finnish President Alexander Stubb noted in an interview with CBS News that Finland and a number of other European countries cannot initially join because they need parliamentary approval to do so in their own countries. 

“One is that this is an international organization which, basically, then needs parliamentary approval. So, you know, we are liberal democracies. We can’t come here and say, ‘okay, here’s the statute,’” Stubb told the network. 

“The other one is that we want to link it even more closer to the U.N. So I think, for instance, that the Gaza peace board is based on a U.N. mandate, which is really good. So now we just need to make sure that some of the other mandates can be put into the U.N. as well. But we’ll see what the other Europeans do and what we do together. I think it’s a good initiative,” he continued. 

The unveiling of the proposal came a day after Trump railed against several European allies in his push for the U.S. to acquire Greenland. That has included increased tensions with French President Emmanuel Macron and U.K Prime Minister Keir Starmer. Several European leaders have been unnerved by the forming of the board and whether it would replace the United Nations Security Council.

Trump and members of his administration, including his son-in-law Jared Kushner, detailed their vision for Gaza’s future, including a presentation that included proposals for an airport, data centers, workforce housing and tourist attractions along the coast of the war-torn enclave.

A Palestinian official named to the newly-formed National Committee for the Administration of Gaza, Dr. Ali Sha’ath, also announced the reopening of the Rafah Crossing, Gaza’s main exit and entry point to and from Egypt. 

During the signing ceremony, Secretary of State Marco Rubio anticipated that more countries would sign onto the Board of Peace charter in the future. 

“Many others who are going to join, you know, others either are not in town today or they have to go through some procedure internally in their own countries, in their own country, because of constitutional limitations, but others will join,” Rubio said.

data.title: Will sale of Nathan's Famous impact July 4 hot dog eating contest?
data.description:
NEW YORK (WPIX) — Smithfield Foods has bought the iconic New York hot dog brand, Nathan’s Famous.

The $450 million all-cash deal means Smithfield will acquire all of Nathan’s outstanding shares at $102 per share. Smithfield has held the rights to produce and sell Nathan’s products in the U.S. and Canada since 2014.

Polish immigrant Nathan Handwerker opened the first Nathan’s hot dog stand in Coney Island in 1916, according to the company. Since then, the company has expanded to include locations in 27 states and Washington, D.C.

However, the notability of the Coney Island boardwalk iconography as host of the annual hot-dog-eating contest may stand above all else.

Does the sale of Nathan’s Famous mean we’ve reached the end of the iconic July 4 hot dog-eating contest in Coney Island? Thankfully, no.

Joey Chestnut wins the men’s competition at Nathan’s Annual Hot Dog Eating Contest on July 4, 2025, in New York City. (Photo by Adam Gray/Getty Images)

According to Smithfield, the event will continue to be hosted at 1229 Boardwalk in Brooklyn as it has since the early 20th Century.

While the first recorded hot dog-eating contest was held in 1972, Nathan’s says informal contests began the year the stand opened early in the 20th century. It says the 2025 contest was its 103rd.

American Joey Chestnut is the reigning Nathan’s hot dog-eating champion after eating 70.5 hot dogs and buns last year. Chestnut has won 17 of the last 19 events, setting a record in 2021 after wolfing down 76 hot dogs and buns.

The future of all Nathan’s locations, meanwhile, was not mentioned in the merger announcement.

Smithfield did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

The Associated Press contributed to this report.
